summary:
Safavid Isfahan (c. 1598–1722 AD) was one of the most beautiful cities in the 17th-century world — the [[event:2338|Safavid Empire's]] capital rebuilt by [[event:2515|Shah Abbas I]] into an imperial showpiece of Shia Islamic architecture, urban planning, and cultural patronage that contemporaries recognized as among the world's great cities and that gave rise to the Persian proverb 'Isfahan is half the world' (Isfahan nesf-e jahan ast). Abbas moved the capital from Qazvin to Isfahan in 1598 and immediately undertook a vast building program: the Naqsh-e Jahan Square (Imam Square) — one of the world's largest public squares — was flanked by the Shah Mosque, the Sheikh Lotfollah Mosque, the Ali Qapu palace, and the Grand Bazaar, creating an ensemble of coordinated Islamic architecture unmatched anywhere.

Safavid Isfahan's cultural achievement extended beyond architecture. The royal workshops produced carpets, ceramics (particularly the blue-and-white tilework that became synonymous with Persian architectural decoration), metalwork, and illustrated manuscripts of extraordinary refinement. European travelers — from the English brothers Robert and Anthony Shirley to the French jeweler Jean-Baptiste Tavernier — wrote admiring accounts of Isfahan's splendor, carrying Persian design motifs back to Europe where they influenced 17th-century decorative arts. The city was estimated to have a population of 500,000 at its height — among the largest in the world at the time. The Afghan invasion of 1722 (which overthrew the last Safavid shah) sacked and partially destroyed Isfahan, ending its era as a great imperial city, though the core monuments survived and the city remains one of Iran's most visited heritage destinations.
